story:
Daddy admired the backyard and told Jacob he was lucky to stay home in the nice weather; Daddy had to go to the office and would miss him. Jacob leaned against his leg to show he’d miss him too. Daddy tousled his hair, warned him not to have too much fun, and to listen to Mommy, who’d be watching from the window. Then he left. Jacob waited a few seconds in case Daddy changed his mind, then stepped onto the lawn.

Their enclosed yard felt like a little paradise: a wooden deck with a table, an umbrella, chairs, and a grill; flowerbeds along the fence; a shady peach tree in one corner and a chicken coop in the other. Two birds landed in the peach tree. Jacob considered what to do first. He would have preferred being inside with Mommy tapping at her computer and the TV on, where snacks and water were close and it wasn’t so hot. But Mommy and Daddy said sunshine was healthy for him, so outside it was.

He picked up a red plastic ball, climbed the steps, dropped it, and watched how far it rolled. Retrieving and dropping it felt like a game, so he did it again and again. Then he turned to the cool, shadowy space under the deck. Small enough to crawl beneath the raised planks, he liked the bugs, old leaves, and secret feeling. He pretended he was hiding from a bear, inching forward, listening for danger, crawling a bit more, until he got bored, wriggled back out, and shook off dirt and leaves.

Just then Mommy opened the door and stepped onto the deck. She didn’t like him under there, so he hurried to her as if he’d been playing elsewhere. “Ah, there you are,” she said. “I’m tired of looking at my computer and talking nice to people. I’ll sit out here a minute.” She sat under the umbrella, told a brief story about her forgetful boss, then stood. “Mommy needs to go back inside. You keep playing. Find some butterflies. I’ll be watching from the window.”

Jacob patrolled the fence line, hunting butterflies and ladybugs among the flowers. He spotted three before he reached the coop corner. Over the clucking, he heard kids laughing on the other side and smelled hamburgers and hot dogs sizzling. He barked a hopeful hello toward the fence. No answer—only a yellow balloon bobbing over it like an invitation. He had to get to the party.

There was no gate he could use and no gaps he hadn’t already checked. That left over, under, or through. The fence was too high to clear. Through seemed possible: one board was looser than the rest. He backed up, ran, ducked his head, and hit shoulder-first. The board didn’t budge. Dizzy, he gave up on through and tried under. He dug at a soft spot but hit a rock, tried another, and found more rocks. The laughter and smells grew stronger. He whined and pawed at the boards. No answer.

He eyed the coop. The chickens were “stupid” and unfriendly, but they were still birds. Maybe if they flew over, the people next door would notice and open their gate, and he could slip in. He knew the spring-loaded door—he wasn’t supposed to touch it—but this felt like an emergency. He nosed the latch the way he’d seen Mommy do; the door sprang and one chicken burst into the yard, then the others. They pecked calmly, ignoring the party. He chased them, hoping to stir wings; they shrieked like he was a bear.

Mommy ran out. “Why are the chickens out? Did you open their door?” Jacob widened his eyes and gave a small head shake. She saw the fresh holes. “Have you been digging again?” Another tiny shake. Laughter drifted over the fence. Jacob inched toward it. Mommy softened. “Nice day for a party. Maybe we’ll have one for lunch. First, help me get the chickens back.”

They herded the birds inside and went into the kitchen. Mommy opened the fridge. “Ice cream? No, that upsets your stomach. How about a hot dog? You like those.” She put one on a plate on the floor. Jacob gobbled it in a few bites. She stroked his fur. “You’re a good boy. You’ve had a busy morning.” He wagged his tail.
